name: ComposeModifiers
tagline: A scrolling set of lessons on the Modifier chain and layout — the two tools you use on every composable.
whatItDoes: You see one vertically-scrolling screen of labelled lessons that make Compose layout VISIBLE with colored tiles. It opens with the headline lesson — 'background → padding' vs 'padding → background' shown side by side, so you can see that swapping two modifiers changes the picture — then a 'border → padding → border' demo that draws two concentric outlines to prove the chain is nested layers. After that it walks through sizing ('size', 'fillMaxWidth(0.5f)', 'wrapContentWidth'), the padding variants, decoration ('clip', 'border', 'shadow'), every 'Arrangement' on its own Row, cross-axis 'Alignment', 'weight' (1 : 2 plus a fixed tile), 'Box' with per-child 'Modifier.align', transforms ('offset', 'alpha', 'rotate'), 'aspectRatio', and finally a 'clickable' tile that counts taps. No navigation, network, or data.
mentalModel: A Modifier is an ORDERED, immutable chain — each call wraps the previous one and Compose applies them outside-in — so the ORDER of '.padding()', '.background()', '.border()' changes the result; layout is then just Column/Row/Box plus Arrangement (main axis), Alignment (cross axis), and weight (sharing leftover space).

screens.name: ModifiersScreen
screens.role: A Scaffold + verticalScroll Column of ~13 labelled lessons, each demonstrating one Modifier or layout idea with colored Tiles.

screens.name: Lesson
screens.role: A reusable frame that draws a title + caption above each demo (the content-slot pattern).

screens.name: Tile
screens.role: A small colored, labelled Box whose 'modifier' parameter is supplied by the caller — so each demo passes in the very modifier it is teaching.

screens.name: MainActivity
screens.role: The single entry point: setContent { ComposeModifiersTheme { ModifiersScreen() } }.

concepts.plain: 'Modifier.a().b().c()' returns a NEW Modifier at each step that WRAPS the previous; Compose applies them outside-in (first = outermost layer). That is why order matters.

'Modifier.background(Blue).padding(16.dp)' fills blue first then insets the text (blue shows AROUND the text); 'Modifier.padding(16.dp).background(Blue)' leaves a transparent margin first then fills (blue HUGS the text). Same two modifiers, different picture.

'size(w, h)' fixes both dimensions; 'fillMaxWidth()' takes the full width and 'fillMaxWidth(0.5f)' takes half; 'wrapContentWidth' shrinks back to the content. 'fillMaxHeight' / 'fillMaxSize' are the height / both variants.

'Arrangement' spreads children along the layout's MAIN axis (vertical for Column, horizontal for Row): 'spacedBy(8.dp)', 'Center', 'SpaceBetween', 'SpaceAround', 'SpaceEvenly'. 'Alignment' lines them up on the CROSS axis: 'horizontalAlignment' in a Column, 'verticalAlignment' in a Row.

Inside a Row/Column, 'Modifier.weight(n)' divides the LEFTOVER main-axis space by ratio. A 'weight(2f)' child is twice as wide as a 'weight(1f)' child; fixed-size children are measured first and weights split what remains. 'weight' only exists inside a RowScope / ColumnScope.

'Box' stacks its children on top of each other; each child calls 'Modifier.align(Alignment.TopStart / Center / BottomEnd / …)' to position itself within the Box. 'align' only exists inside a BoxScope.

'clip(CircleShape)' rounds, 'border(width, color, shape)' outlines, 'shadow(elevation, shape)' lifts off the surface; 'offset' nudges, 'alpha' fades, 'rotate' spins, and 'aspectRatio(16f/9f)' locks a width:height ratio.

files.role: Every lesson, plus the reusable Lesson frame and the Tile helper, MainActivity, and two @Preview functions.
ComposeModifiersTheme — the Material 3 wrapper supplying colorScheme + typography.
Plain-English summary of the one big idea (the ordered chain), every lesson, and what to inspect.

highlights.title: Tile — a reusable composable that takes a modifier parameter
highlights.explain: 'Tile(label, color, modifier)' draws a centered white label on a colored Box and forwards the caller's 'modifier' to that Box. Accepting a 'modifier: Modifier = Modifier' parameter is the #1 convention for reusable composables — here it lets every lesson pass in the very modifier it is teaching (e.g. 'Tile("A", color, Modifier.weight(1f))').

highlights.title: The chain is read outside-in
highlights.explain: 'Modifier.size(...).clip(...).background(...).padding(...)' is applied in order: reserve the space, round the corners, fill the (now-rounded) area, then inset the content. Each call wraps the previous one; the first in the chain is the outermost layer.

highlights.title: Order matters: background vs padding (the headline)
highlights.explain: The two boxes use the SAME two modifiers in the opposite order. 'Modifier.background(Blue).padding(16.dp)' fills blue then insets the text, so blue shows around the text. 'Modifier.padding(16.dp).background(Blue)' leaves a transparent 16dp margin (the card shows through) then fills, so blue hugs the text. Sitting both on a surfaceVariant card makes the transparent padding obvious.

highlights.title: weight — share leftover space by ratio
highlights.explain: In the Row, 'Modifier.weight(2f)' ends up twice as wide as 'Modifier.weight(1f)', while the fixed 'Modifier.width(48.dp)' tile is measured first and the weights divide what remains. 'fillMaxHeight()' makes each tile as tall as the 44dp Row so the proportions read clearly.

highlights.title: Box & Modifier.align — position children in a stack
highlights.explain: 'Box' overlaps its children; each Tile uses 'Modifier.align(Alignment.TopStart / Center / BottomEnd)' to sit in a different spot of the same box. 'align' is a BoxScope extension — it only exists (and only compiles) inside a Box's content lambda.

highlights.title: MainActivity wires it up
highlights.explain: The single entry point: 'onCreate' calls 'enableEdgeToEdge()' then 'setContent { ComposeModifiersTheme { ModifiersScreen() } }'. No navigation, no data — it just installs the scrolling lesson screen.

highlights.title: The headline lesson as a focused @Preview
highlights.explain: 'OrderMattersPreview' renders just the background-vs-padding comparison in the design pane. Because it is pure UI with literal colors, it renders instantly without an emulator — the fastest way to stare at the difference until the idea sticks.

newVsPrev: ComposeCatalog showed you WHICH component to reach for; ComposeModifiers shows you how to CONTROL it. It isolates the two tools you apply to every composable: the Modifier chain (and its all-important ORDER) and layout (Column/Row/Box + Arrangement, Alignment, weight, align). Where the catalog's demos mostly used default modifiers, this project makes layout itself the subject — colored tiles, side-by-side 'order matters' comparisons, every Arrangement on its own row — so the rules become visible. Together the two projects are the 'which widget' and 'how to arrange it' halves of building a Compose screen.
tryThis: In the headline lesson, swap '.background(Blue).padding(16.dp)' to '.padding(16.dp).background(Blue)' on the first box and watch the blue move from around the text to hugging it.
In the 'weight' lesson, change 'weight(2f)' to 'weight(3f)' and watch that tile grow relative to the 'weight(1f)' one; then add a second fixed-width tile and see the weights re-divide what's left.
In the 'Arrangement' lesson, add a row using 'Arrangement.spacedBy(24.dp)' and compare it to 'SpaceBetween'.
In the 'Box & align' lesson, change one Tile's 'Alignment.BottomEnd' to 'Alignment.TopCenter' and see it jump to a new corner.
Open MainActivity.kt in Android Studio and look at the 'Order: background vs padding' @Preview — the difference is obvious at a glance with no emulator.
Reproduce the same experiments live in Playground/playground.html: type a Modifier chain, reorder '.padding()' and '.background()', and watch the preview re-render instantly.
gotchas: 'Modifier.weight(...)' and 'Modifier.align(...)' are SCOPED extensions: 'weight' only exists inside a RowScope/ColumnScope and 'align' inside a BoxScope. They compile only because the calls sit lexically inside those layout lambdas.

Order is significant for paint/space modifiers (padding, background, border, clip, shadow) but generally not for the others — reordering '.background()' and '.padding()' changes the look; reordering two independent decorations usually doesn't.
'TopAppBar' is experimental, so 'ModifiersScreen' is annotated '@OptIn(ExperimentalMaterial3Api::class)', just like ComposeCatalog.
'shadow(elevation, shape)' is applied BEFORE 'background(color, shape)' so the shadow follows the rounded outline; flip them and the shadow can be clipped away.
Tiles use raw 'Color(0xFF…)' values (Blue/Green/Orange/Purple) rather than theme colors, on purpose, so the demos have predictable contrast in both light and dark.
runTest: Build and install from the project root with './gradlew installDebug' (or './gradlew assembleDebug' to just build the APK), then scroll the lessons and tap the 'clickable' tile to watch its counter rise. Run the example unit test with './gradlew testDebugUnitTest'. Fastest of all: open MainActivity.kt in Android Studio and view the two @Preview functions — the 'Order: background vs padding' preview makes the headline lesson obvious without an emulator. There are no deep links in this project.
